text: A 65-year-old man has sudden crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm with ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF indicates an inferior STEMI, usually involving the right coronary artery. This is a medical emergency requiring reperfusion.

text: A 72-year-old man with hypertension has an S4 heart sound. What does this most likely indicate?
rationale: An S4 occurs when the atrium contracts against a stiff noncompliant ventricle. It is commonly associated with longstanding hypertension and left ventricular hypertrophy.

text: A 30-year-old woman has episodic palpitations. ECG during symptoms shows narrow-complex tachycardia at 180/min that terminates with adenosine. Diagnosis?
rationale: AV nodal re-entrant tachycardia is a common narrow-complex supraventricular tachycardia and often terminates with adenosine. Vagal maneuvers are also useful.

text: A 24-year-old IV drug user has fever and a new holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border. Most likely valve involved in infective endocarditis?
rationale: In IV drug users, infective endocarditis most commonly involves the tricuspid valve. Staphylococcus aureus is a major causative organism.

text: A 22-year-old man has fever, nonproductive cough, headache, and patchy infiltrates on chest X-ray. He is otherwise healthy. Most likely organism?
rationale: Mycoplasma pneumoniae causes atypical pneumonia in young adults with dry cough, headache, and patchy infiltrates. It is often called walking pneumonia.

text: A 58-year-old smoker presents with chronic productive cough for at least 3 months per year over 2 consecutive years. Diagnosis?
rationale: Chronic bronchitis is defined clinically by productive cough for at least 3 months per year for 2 consecutive years. Smoking is the major risk factor.

text: A 67-year-old man with COPD exacerbation has increased sputum purulence and dyspnea. Best initial inhaled bronchodilator?
rationale: Acute COPD exacerbations are initially treated with short-acting bronchodilators such as salbutamol. Additional treatment may include steroids and antibiotics when indicated.

text: A 35-year-old woman has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and erythema nodosum. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Sarcoidosis often presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and noncaseating granulomas. It commonly affects young to middle-aged adults.

text: A 50-year-old obese woman has burning retrosternal pain worse after meals and when lying down. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Gastroesophageal reflux disease typically causes heartburn that worsens after meals and in the supine position. Obesity is a major risk factor.

text: A 45-year-old man with cirrhosis becomes confused and has asterixis. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hepatic encephalopathy presents with confusion, asterixis, and altered mental status in advanced liver disease. Ammonia accumulation contributes to symptoms.

text: A patient with ulcerative colitis is at increased risk for which malignancy?
rationale: Longstanding ulcerative colitis increases the risk of colorectal cancer. Surveillance colonoscopy is important in chronic disease.

text: A 30-year-old man has dysphagia to both solids and liquids. Barium swallow shows bird-beak narrowing. Diagnosis?
rationale: Achalasia causes dysphagia to both solids and liquids due to failure of LES relaxation. Bird-beak appearance on barium swallow is classic.

text: A 28-year-old woman has unilateral throbbing headache with photophobia and nausea. Symptoms improve in a dark room. Diagnosis?
rationale: Migraine commonly causes unilateral throbbing headache with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Patients often prefer a dark quiet room.

text: A 45-year-old man has severe unilateral periorbital headaches with tearing and nasal congestion. Attacks last 45 minutes. Diagnosis?
rationale: Cluster headache causes brief, severe unilateral periorbital pain with autonomic symptoms like lacrimation and nasal congestion. It is more common in men.

text: A 55-year-old woman has ascending weakness and areflexia after a recent infection. Best treatment?
rationale: Guillain-Barré syndrome is treated with IVIG or plasmapheresis. Steroids alone are not effective as primary therapy.

text: A 33-year-old woman develops sudden monocular vision loss with pain on eye movement. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Optic neuritis presents with painful monocular vision loss and is strongly associated with multiple sclerosis. It is a classic early manifestation.

text: A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing, and ketones. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: DKA presents with hyperglycemia, ketonemia, metabolic acidosis, and Kussmaul respirations, especially in type 1 diabetes. It requires urgent fluids, insulin, and electrolyte correction.

text: An elderly man with type 2 diabetes presents with glucose 42 mmol/L, profound dehydration, and no ketones. Diagnosis?
rationale: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state presents with extreme hyperglycemia, severe dehydration, and minimal or absent ketones. It is more common in type 2 diabetes.

text: A 36-year-old woman has fatigue, weight loss, tremor, and low TSH. Which lab pattern is most consistent with primary hyperthyroidism?
rationale: Primary hyperthyroidism typically shows suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 or T3. Graves disease is a common cause.

text: A 24-year-old woman has fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, and hyperkalemia. Best diagnosis?
rationale: Primary adrenal insufficiency causes low cortisol and aldosterone, leading to hypotension, hyperkalemia, and hyperpigmentation from elevated ACTH.

text: A 70-year-old diabetic has proteinuria and gradually rising creatinine over years. Most likely cause of CKD?
rationale: Diabetic nephropathy is the most common cause of chronic kidney disease in many populations. Persistent proteinuria is a key feature.

text: A 28-year-old woman has dysuria, frequency, and no flank pain or fever. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Acute uncomplicated cystitis presents with dysuria and frequency without systemic symptoms. It usually lacks flank pain and fever.

text: A patient has hyperkalemia with peaked T waves on ECG. Immediate stabilizing treatment?
rationale: IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane in dangerous hyperkalemia. Other measures help shift or remove potassium.

text: A 40-year-old woman has recurrent hematuria and bilateral enlarged kidneys. Family history is positive. Diagnosis?
rationale: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease presents with enlarged kidneys, hematuria, hypertension, and family history. Berry aneurysms are associated.

text: A 32-year-old woman has fatigue and microcytosis. Ferritin is low. Best diagnosis?
rationale: Low ferritin strongly suggests iron deficiency anemia. It typically causes microcytic hypochromic anemia.

text: A 70-year-old man has lymphocytosis and smudge cells on blood smear. Diagnosis?
rationale: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia commonly presents in older adults with lymphocytosis and smudge cells. It is often indolent at diagnosis.

text: A 25-year-old woman has fever, neurologic symptoms, thrombocytopenia, and hemolytic anemia. Diagnosis?
rationale: TTP classically presents with thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, and fever. Plasma exchange is urgent treatment.

text: A 60-year-old man has bone pain, anemia, renal dysfunction, and elevated total protein. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Multiple myeloma causes CRAB features: hyperCalcemia, Renal dysfunction, Anemia, and Bone lesions. Monoclonal protein is typical.

text: A 27-year-old woman has malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, and arthritis. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Systemic lupus erythematosus commonly presents with malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, and arthritis. Multisystem involvement is common.

text: A 62-year-old woman has dry eyes and dry mouth with positive anti-Ro antibodies. Diagnosis?
rationale: Sjögren syndrome causes sicca symptoms such as dry eyes and dry mouth. Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies may be present.

text: A 68-year-old man has acute swollen painful knee. Joint aspiration shows positively birefringent rhomboid crystals. Diagnosis?
rationale: Pseudogout is caused by calcium pyrophosphate deposition and shows positively birefringent rhomboid crystals. It often affects older adults.

text: A 30-year-old man has low back pain improving with exercise, reduced chest expansion, and sacroiliitis. Diagnosis?
rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis causes inflammatory back pain, reduced spinal mobility, and sacroiliitis. Symptoms often improve with exercise rather than rest.

text: A child has honey-colored crusted lesions around the mouth. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Impetigo commonly presents with honey-colored crusts, often around the mouth and nose. It is commonly caused by Staph aureus or Group A Strep.

text: A 19-year-old woman has intensely pruritic burrows between the fingers. Diagnosis?
rationale: Scabies causes intense itching and burrows, especially in finger webs, wrists, and groin. It is treated with permethrin.

text: A 7-year-old boy has annular scaly plaque with central clearing on the arm. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tinea corporis presents as an annular scaly lesion with central clearing. It is a superficial dermatophyte infection.

text: A patient has depigmented sharply demarcated patches on the hands and face. Diagnosis?
rationale: Vitiligo causes depigmented patches due to autoimmune loss of melanocytes. It often affects acral and facial areas.

text: A 19-year-old college student presents with fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and purpuric rash. Most likely organism?
rationale: Meningococcal meningitis can present with fever, neck stiffness, and petechial or purpuric rash, especially in young adults in close quarters.

text: A patient with suspected bacterial meningitis needs antibiotics. Best principle?
options: Treat only if CSF glucose normal
Avoid antibiotics before LP in all cases
rationale: In suspected bacterial meningitis, empiric antibiotics should be started promptly because delay worsens outcomes. Diagnostic steps should not create unsafe delay.

text: A 35-year-old man has dysuria and urethral discharge. Gram stain shows intracellular gram-negative diplococci. Diagnosis?
rationale: Neisseria gonorrhoeae appears as intracellular gram-negative diplococci in urethral discharge. It commonly causes urethritis.

text: A patient has fever and a new murmur. Blood cultures grow viridans streptococci after recent dental work. Diagnosis?
rationale: Viridans streptococci after dental manipulation suggest infective endocarditis, especially in patients with abnormal valves. Multiple blood cultures support diagnosis.

text: A 40-year-old woman has low mood, anhedonia, guilt, poor concentration, and early morning awakening for 2 months. Diagnosis?
rationale: Major depressive disorder requires at least 2 weeks of depressive symptoms including low mood or anhedonia. Functional impairment is common.

text: A 26-year-old man has elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, and risky behavior for 8 days. Diagnosis?
rationale: Mania lasts at least 1 week or requires hospitalization and includes elevated mood, decreased sleep, grandiosity, and risky behavior. It defines bipolar I disorder.

text: A 29-year-old woman has intrusive thoughts of contamination and repeated handwashing rituals. Diagnosis?
rationale: Obsessive-compulsive disorder involves intrusive obsessions and repetitive compulsions that the patient recognizes as excessive or irrational.

text: A 30-year-old man has recurrent unexpected panic attacks and avoids leaving home. This avoidance behavior is called?
rationale: Agoraphobia is fear or avoidance of situations where escape may be difficult, commonly associated with panic disorder.

text: A 22-year-old man has hallucinations, delusions, and social withdrawal for 1 month after a major stressor. Symptoms resolve completely by 2 months. Diagnosis?
rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts more than 1 day but less than 1 month, often related to stress, and is followed by full return to baseline.

text: A patient is brought in intoxicated with slurred speech, ataxia, and nystagmus after drinking heavily. Most likely substance?
rationale: Alcohol intoxication commonly causes slurred speech, ataxia, and nystagmus. Severe intoxication can lead to respiratory depression.

text: A term newborn develops jaundice on day 3 of life, feeds well, and otherwise appears healthy. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Physiologic jaundice typically appears after the first 24 hours, often around day 2 to 3, in otherwise healthy newborns. It is due to immature bilirubin metabolism.

text: A preterm infant is at highest risk of which complication due to prematurity of retinal vessels?
rationale: Retinopathy of prematurity is a complication in preterm infants related to immature retinal vasculature, particularly with oxygen exposure.

text: An infant with RSV bronchiolitis is wheezing and feeding poorly. Main treatment principle?
rationale: Bronchiolitis is mainly treated with supportive care including oxygen and hydration when needed. RSV is the most common cause.

text: A 3-year-old suddenly starts coughing and wheezing while playing with peanuts. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Sudden cough and wheeze during eating or play strongly suggest foreign body aspiration. Bronchoscopy is often required.

text: A 6-year-old has cola-colored urine, periorbital edema, and hypertension 2 weeks after sore throat. Diagnosis?
rationale: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis causes hematuria, edema, and hypertension after a recent streptococcal infection.

text: A 4-year-old has edema, heavy proteinuria, and hypoalbuminemia. Most common diagnosis in children?
rationale: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. It often responds well to steroids.

text: A 3-year-old has delayed speech, poor eye contact, and repetitive behaviors. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Autism spectrum disorder is characterized by deficits in social communication and restricted repetitive behaviors. Early intervention is important.

text: A child develops barking cough and inspiratory stridor at night. Most likely viral cause?
rationale: Croup is most commonly caused by parainfluenza virus. It presents with barking cough and inspiratory stridor.

text: A child has fever and sore throat. Exam shows tender anterior cervical nodes, tonsillar exudates, and no cough. Best diagnosis?
rationale: This presentation fits Group A streptococcal pharyngitis, especially with fever, exudates, tender anterior nodes, and no cough.

text: A newborn has machine-like continuous murmur. Diagnosis?
rationale: Patent ductus arteriosus classically causes a continuous machine-like murmur. It is more common in premature infants.

text: A child with Tetralogy of Fallot becomes cyanotic during crying. Best immediate maneuver?
rationale: Tet spells can improve with the knee-chest position, which increases systemic vascular resistance and reduces right-to-left shunting.

text: A 26-year-old woman has curdy white vaginal discharge and intense itching. Vaginal pH is normal. Diagnosis?
rationale: Candida vaginitis causes pruritus and curdy white discharge, often with a normal vaginal pH. It is commonly treated with azole therapy.

text: A 32-year-old woman has irregular menses, infertility, obesity, and hirsutism. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Polycystic ovary syndrome commonly presents with irregular periods, infertility, obesity, and hyperandrogenic features like hirsutism.

text: A postmenopausal woman presents with an adnexal mass, bloating, and early satiety. Most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Ovarian cancer may present with vague symptoms such as bloating, early satiety, and adnexal mass, especially in postmenopausal women.

text: A pregnant woman has seizures in the setting of severe preeclampsia. Diagnosis?
rationale: Eclampsia is defined by new-onset seizures in a woman with preeclampsia. Magnesium sulfate is central treatment and prevention.

text: A 12-week pregnant woman has severe vomiting, ketonuria, and weight loss. Diagnosis?
rationale: Hyperemesis gravidarum causes severe persistent vomiting in pregnancy with dehydration, weight loss, and ketonuria.

text: A mother is Rh-negative and delivers an Rh-positive infant. What prevents alloimmunization?
rationale: Anti-D immunoglobulin is given to Rh-negative mothers to prevent sensitization to Rh-positive fetal blood cells.

text: A 23-year-old man has RLQ pain, rebound tenderness, fever, and leukocytosis. Best treatment?
rationale: Acute appendicitis is usually treated with appendectomy. Delayed treatment increases perforation risk.

text: A 48-year-old woman has symptomatic gallstones causing biliary colic. Definitive management?
rationale: Symptomatic gallstones are definitively treated with laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Biliary colic often recurs without surgery.

text: A patient has colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, and no bowel movements. Diagnosis?
rationale: Bowel obstruction classically presents with colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, and obstipation.

text: A trauma patient is hypotensive with absent breath sounds on the left and tracheal deviation to the right. Diagnosis?
rationale: Tension pneumothorax causes hypotension, unilateral absent breath sounds, and tracheal deviation away from the affected side. It requires immediate decompression.

text: An elderly woman falls and has shortened externally rotated leg. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Hip fracture often presents with a shortened externally rotated leg after a fall in an older patient. Early surgery and mobilization are important.

text: A football player twists his knee and hears a pop, followed by swelling and instability. Most likely injured structure?
rationale: An ACL tear commonly occurs with twisting injury, pop sensation, rapid swelling, and knee instability.

text: A man has painless hematuria and is a long-term smoker. Most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Painless hematuria in an older smoker suggests bladder cancer until proven otherwise. Cystoscopy is key for evaluation.

text: A patient has sudden painful red eye, halos around lights, and fixed mid-dilated pupil. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with painful red eye, halos, headache, and a fixed mid-dilated pupil. It is an ophthalmic emergency.

text: A diabetic patient has gradually progressive blurry vision and fundus exam shows microaneurysms. Diagnosis?
rationale: Diabetic retinopathy often begins with microaneurysms and can progress to hemorrhages, exudates, and neovascularization.

text: A child has ear pain, fever, and bulging erythematous tympanic membrane. Diagnosis?
rationale: Acute otitis media presents with ear pain, fever, and a bulging erythematous tympanic membrane. It often follows an upper respiratory infection.

text: A swimmer develops severe ear pain with tragal tenderness. Diagnosis?
rationale: Otitis externa causes ear pain with tragal tenderness and often follows water exposure. Pseudomonas is a common organism.

text: A 55-year-old smoker has persistent hoarseness for 3 months. Most concerning diagnosis?
rationale: Persistent hoarseness in a smoker raises concern for laryngeal cancer. It requires visualization of the larynx.

text: A 52-year-old man with 25 pack-year smoking history still smokes. Recommended lung cancer screening?
rationale: Low-dose CT is the recommended screening test for eligible high-risk adults because it detects lung cancer earlier than chest X-ray.

text: Which vaccine is routinely recommended annually for most adults?
rationale: Influenza vaccine is recommended annually for most adults because circulating strains change over time.

text: A 66-year-old man who smoked should be screened once for which condition with ultrasound?
rationale: One-time abdominal ultrasound screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm is recommended in certain older men with smoking history.

text: A 45-year-old woman asks for first-line treatment for uncomplicated hypertension without compelling indications. Reasonable initial class?
rationale: ACE inhibitors are a common first-line option for uncomplicated hypertension, depending on patient factors and local guidelines.

text: A 50-year-old man has LDL cholesterol 5.2 mmol/L with cardiovascular risk factors. Main medication class for risk reduction?
rationale: Statins lower LDL cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk. They are central therapy in dyslipidemia management.

text: A patient is unconscious with pinpoint pupils and respiratory depression. Most likely overdose?
rationale: Opioid overdose classically causes CNS depression, pinpoint pupils, and respiratory depression. Naloxone is the antidote.

text: A 19-year-old with peanut allergy develops wheeze, hypotension, and generalized urticaria after eating dessert. First treatment?
rationale: Anaphylaxis requires immediate IM epinephrine as first-line treatment. Airway and circulation support are also essential.

text: A burned patient has hoarseness and soot around the mouth after house fire. Most urgent concern?
rationale: Facial burns, soot, and hoarseness suggest inhalation injury and impending airway compromise. Early airway management is critical.

text: A patient overdoses on acetaminophen. Antidote?
rationale: N-acetylcysteine is the antidote for acetaminophen toxicity and is most effective when given early.

text: A farmer has salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, and bronchospasm after pesticide exposure. Toxidrome?
rationale: Organophosphate poisoning causes a cholinergic toxidrome with salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI upset, and bronchospasm.

text: Best antidote pair for organophosphate poisoning?
rationale: Organophosphate poisoning is treated with atropine to block muscarinic effects and pralidoxime to reactivate acetylcholinesterase.

text: A competent adult refuses a recommended surgery after understanding the risks. Most appropriate action?
rationale: A competent adult has the right to refuse recommended treatment, even if refusal increases risk of harm or death.

text: A 16-year-old asks for contraception and demonstrates understanding. Most appropriate principle?
rationale: A mature minor who demonstrates capacity may consent to confidential medical care, depending on jurisdiction and circumstances.

text: A physician notices a medication error that caused no patient harm. Best action?
rationale: Professional ethics support disclosure and documentation of medical errors and near misses, even when no harm occurred.

text: A delirious patient refuses urgent treatment. What must be assessed first?
rationale: Refusal of treatment requires assessment of decision-making capacity. Delirium commonly impairs capacity.

text: A 45-year-old man with obesity and daytime somnolence snores loudly at night. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with obesity, loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and daytime sleepiness.

text: A 48-year-old woman has chronic pruritus, cholestatic liver enzymes, and positive antimitochondrial antibodies. Diagnosis?
rationale: Primary biliary cholangitis is an autoimmune cholestatic disease associated with pruritus and positive antimitochondrial antibodies.

text: A man with ulcerative colitis is found to have beading of the bile ducts on imaging. Associated diagnosis?
rationale: Primary sclerosing cholangitis is associated with ulcerative colitis and causes multifocal stricturing and beading of bile ducts.

text: A 20-year-old tall man has lens dislocation and aortic root dilation. Diagnosis?
rationale: Marfan syndrome is associated with tall stature, ectopia lentis, and aortic root disease due to fibrillin-1 mutation.

text: A short woman with webbed neck and primary amenorrhea is most likely to have which syndrome?
rationale: Turner syndrome commonly presents with short stature, webbed neck, and gonadal dysgenesis causing primary amenorrhea.

text: A 12-month-old should most likely be able to do which milestone?
options: Say at least one meaningful word
rationale: Around 12 months, many infants say at least one meaningful word and may begin to stand or cruise. Full sentence speech comes much later.

text: A 2-year-old child most commonly should be able to do which milestone?
rationale: By around 2 years, children commonly use 2-word phrases. Language delay beyond this point may warrant evaluation.

text: A 74-year-old man has gait difficulty, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline. MRI shows enlarged ventricles. Diagnosis?
rationale: Normal pressure hydrocephalus classically presents with gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, and cognitive decline. Ventricular enlargement supports the diagnosis.

text: A patient hears voices commanding self-harm and appears frightened. Most important immediate priority?
rationale: Command hallucinations raise immediate safety concerns, so urgent assessment of suicidality and risk to self or others is essential.

text: A patient with long-standing smoking history asks for the single most effective intervention to reduce future COPD progression risk. Best answer?
rationale: Smoking cessation is the most effective intervention to slow COPD progression and reduce future respiratory decline.
